segments.s605-g33: برانگیخت is a past, third‑person singular perfective verb (poetic form) meaning 'roused/raised.' In this poetic word order the verb comes first, followed by its object اسپ (the steed). از میان گروه is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the midst of the company' specifying where the horse was roused from.
segments.s605-g34: This is a motion clause: ز introduces source 'from the plain,' همی is the classical/prose‑poetic progressive marker (here giving a continuous or iterative sense to the past action), and تاخت is the past verb 'galloped/rushed.' تا ... پیش (here تا + پیش + کوه) marks the limit/destination 'as far as / up to before the mountain.'
segments.s605-g35: فرود آمد is a compound past tense 'he alighted/descended' (فرود + آمد). The following phrase از باره جایی نهفت contains the object/action: باره = load/baggage, جایی = 'somewhere' (adverb), and نهفت is the past verb 'he hid/concealed.' Word order places the descriptive/location elements before the verb, typical in literary Persian.
segments.s605-g36: یله کرد is a light‑verb construction: یله 'slack/loose' + کرد 'made/did' = 'he loosened' (implied the girth/reins). The conjunction و links this to در سایه کوه خفت where در سایه کوه is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the shade of the mountain' and خفت is the past 'he slept.'
segments.s605-g37: ز کوه gives the source 'from the mountain.' اندرآمد here functions as a past motion verb 'came down/approached' (poetic inversion places the verb before the subject), and کلاغ سیاه is the subject 'a black crow' which follows the verb for stylistic effect.
segments.s605-g38: دو چشمش is the object 'his two eyes' (two + noun + possessive). بکند is the verbal form here meaning 'did/caused' (the crow performed the action); اندران خوابگاه is a locative/source phrase 'out of/from that sleeping‑place' (اندران = 'therein/from within it' + خوابگاه = 'sleeping‑place'). The object preceding the verb and the locative phrase after it reflect poetic ordering.
segments.s605-g39: همی is the classical progressive particle (here giving a continuous past sense), تاختند is the past plural 'they galloped/rushed.' از پس‌اندر گروه is a prepositional/advantage phrase 'from/after behind the company' indicating they pursued or followed the group — پس‌اندر here = 'after/behind.'
segments.s605-g40: ورا is an adverb meaning 'there/thither' (locative), مرده is 'the dead one/corpse' (noun), and دیدند is the past plural 'they saw.' بر پیش کوه is a locative phrase ('on/upon before the mountain') meaning 'at the foot/in front of the mountain' — بر expresses location and پیش کوه specifies the spot.
segments.s605-g49: رخ شهریار جهان is a nominal phrase where رخ (face) is the head and شهریار جهان (the sovereign of the world) is a genitive/possessive chain; the ezāfe is omitted as is common in poetry. زرد شد is a simple past/resultative: زرد (pale/wan) + شد (became).
segments.s605-g50: ز تیمار کبروی is a causal/agentive phrase ('because of / from the care for Kubrūy') with ز = 'because of/from' and تیمار کبروی = 'care for Kubrūy' (ezāfe again omitted). پر درد شد is a light-predicate: پر = 'full (of)', درد = 'pain/sorrow', شد = 'became' — 'became full of pain.'
segments.s605-g51: هم‌انگه is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that moment' before the verb. برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth.' ز درگه خروش is an ablative phrase 'from the court a cry' (ز = 'from', درگه = 'royal court', خروش = 'cry/uproar').
segments.s605-g52: که‌ای here is an exclamatory/vocative particle ('O' — classically written که ای), addressing the following noun; نامداران is the vocative 'renowned ones.' با فر و هوش is a prepositional phrase 'with (royal) glory and intelligence' (با = 'with', فر = 'royal glory', هوش = 'intellect/awareness').
segments.s605-g53: حرامست here functions as a label/descriptor meaning 'a scoundrel' (poetic/archaic form). می‌در is a verbal form 'holds/rules' (present/habitual sense used narratively). جهان سربسر is an adverbial phrase 'throughout the world' or 'in every part of the world' (سربسر = 'everywhere/entirely'). The whole clause reads 'A scoundrel rules the whole world' (with an evaluative tone).
segments.s605-g54: The clause is concessive/conditional: اگر ... گر functions like 'even if' or 'if — yet'. زیردستت‌گر combines زیردستت ('your subordinate', with the enclitic ـت 'your') and the concessive/particle گر; نامور means 'renowned/famed.' So the segment means 'even if he is your famed subordinate.'
segments.s605-g41: This phrase gives the patient and the action in poetic (inverted) order: دو چشمش (his two eyes) is the object, ز سر کنده is a past participial/verbal phrase meaning “torn off/from the head” (ز marks source), and زاغ سیاه (the black crow) is the agent placed after the verb as is common in poetry. کنده is the resultative/past participle of کندن used here as the main action.
segments.s605-g42: برش here is an archaic/poetic cluster (literally 'بر' + enclitic 'ش') functioning like 'in front of him' / 'before him' (not the noun 'cut'); اسپ او (his steed) uses the separate pronoun او after the noun instead of a suffix; ایستاده is a participle meaning 'standing' (resultative state), and به راه = 'on the road' (به + راه indicates location/direction).
segments.s605-g43: برو is an imperative/interjection here ('Go!' or 'away!') used as an exclamation at the start of the clause; کهترانش is a plural noun with the possessive/enclitic ش 'his' ('his inferiors' or lesser men); خروشان is an adjective/participle 'roaring/tumultuous' used predicatively, and شدند is the past plural auxiliary 'became' marking the change of state.
segments.s605-g44: وزان is a compound linking adverb 'and from that / because of that' introducing the consequence; مجلس و جام is a coordinate subject ('the assembly/court and the cups') and جوشان شدند is a predicate (جوشان 'boiling/ seething' + شدند 'they became') — here metaphorical, indicating uproar/excitement.
segments.s605-g45: چو + past verb (برخاست) is a temporal clause 'when Bahram rose'; بهرام is the subject name; از خوابگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the sleeping-place/quarters' indicating where he rose from. The tense is simple past (narrative).
segments.s605-g46: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd sing.). بر او is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto him' (here 'to him/approached him'); یکی نیک‌خواه is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain well-wisher' (یکی = someone/a certain one, followed by the appositive نیک‌خواه). Word order places the visitor after the verb, a common poetic/narrative pattern.
segments.s605-g47: که here introduces the content/relative clause 'that' (reporting what was said). کبروی را uses the object marker را on the name/term کبروی (here a definite/personal reference), while چشم روشن ('bright eye') precedes the verb phrase in inverted poetic order; کلاغ is the agent 'the crow' placed at the end as is typical in poetry.
segments.s605-g48: ز مستی = 'from/because of drunkenness' (ز marks cause); بکندست is an archaic/past form of کندن 'to pull/tear out' (perfective past; poetic inflection), here meaning 'had plucked out'; در پیش is a locative phrase 'in front of / before', and راغ is the referent present there (likely a name); the whole phrase gives the circumstance/place of the action.
segments.s605-g9: همین (this/this very) emphasizes the following noun; مه is the poetic form of ماه 'moon' used here as a conventional epithet for a beautiful face. که introduces a relative clause ('who/which'), با + noun is a prepositional phrase 'with [fruit and scent]', the conjunction و links میوه and بوی, and بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole segment means 'this very moon who was with fruit and fragrance.'
segments.s605-g10: ورا here is a bound pronoun meaning 'to/at her' (used with spatial words); پهلوی is a noun meaning 'side/beside', so ورا پهلوی = 'beside her'. نام is used as 'name'/'reputation' and کبروی is an adjective 'great/supreme' modifying نام; بود is the past copula 'was'. Together: 'beside her was a great name' (i.e. she had a lofty reputation).
segments.s605-g11: به روی + [NP] is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before / at the face of' (here 'before the sovereign'); the head NP is روی جهاندار 'the sovereign's face' (word order: preposition + noun + title). جام نبید is a noun phrase 'a cup (of) wine' where نبید ('wine') follows جام.
segments.s605-g12: دو من را is the definite object (را marks the object) and here من is a measure (a 'man', a quantity of liquid) — 'two mans' — and به یکبار is an adverbial phrase 'at one time / in one go'. اندر کشید is a poetic/perf. phrasing of کشیدن used here to mean 'drew in / swallowed'; note that the supplied senses list for من and کشید did not include the measure or the verb sense 'to drink/draw in', so those senses are not represented in the provided inventory.
segments.s605-g13: چو + شد forms a temporal clause 'when he became'; مرد خرم is the subject NP 'the man (mard) joyful (khurram)'. ز + دیدار شاه gives the cause/reason 'from / because of the meeting/sight of the king' (z is the classical preposition 'from/because of'). The clause means 'when the man became joyful at seeing the king.'
segments.s605-g14: ازان here means 'from that / because of that' and links to the previous causal phrase; نامداران (literally 'the famed ones') functions as a plural noun 'those renowned men', و آن جشنگاه coordinates and adds 'and that place/occasion of festivity'. The segment further specifies the source of the man's joy: 'because of those illustrious men and that festive place.'
segments.s605-g15: یکی before a noun can be an indefinite determiner 'one / a certain'; جام دیگر is 'another cup' (دیگر after the noun often means 'another'). پر از is the idiom 'full of', and می‌بلور is a poetic word related to 'crystal'/'crystallize' used adjectivally here to mean 'crystal-bright' or 'sparkling'. The phrase describes 'another goblet, full and sparkling.'
segments.s605-g16: به دلش اندر افتاد is an idiom 'fell into his heart' meaning 'it seized/struck his heart' or 'took hold of him emotionally' (به = to/into, دلش = his heart, اندر افتاد = 'fell into/entered'). زان جام شور: زان = 'from that', جام = 'cup', شور = 'passion/ardor' — 'from that cup (came) passion'. Note شور is used here in the emotional sense 'ardor'.
segments.s605-g17: ز پیش بزرگان بیازید دست is a prepositional phrase + verb phrase in inverted (poetic) word order: ز پیش بزرگان = "in the presence of / before the elders" (ز = "from/before", پیش = "in the presence of", بزرگان = "the great ones/elders"). بیازید (archaic/poetic past form) governs دست and means "(they) stretched/opened" — here: "they stretched out their hands." دست is the object/what is stretched out. The overall clause uses an older past/poetic form (بیازید) rather than the modern بیازیدند or دراز کردند.
segments.s605-g18: بدان جام می‌تاخت و بر پای جست contains بدَان (ب + دان) = "toward that/unto that" (ب as the preposition "to/toward", دان as demonstrative "that" in poetic spelling), جام = "cup", می‌تاخت = present‑progressive/poetic imperfect of تاختن meaning "rushed" or "bounded" (here narrative past sense: 'he rushed'), و = coordinating "and", بر پای جست = "leapt to his feet" with بر = "onto/upon", پای = "foot/feet" and جست (past of جستن) = "he leapt/sprang." Note the compacted prepositional prefix (ب-) and poetic forms of the verbs.
segments.s605-g19: به یاد شهنشاه بگرفت جام: به یاد = "in memory of / thinking of" (preposition + noun functioning adverbially), شهنشاه = "the emperor/king", بگرفت = poetic past of گرفتن "he seized/took hold of", جام = "the cup" (direct object). The clause order is topical ("in remembrance of the king" fronted) followed by the main verb and object.
segments.s605-g20: منم گفت میخواره کبروی نام: گفت here introduces direct speech; منم is emphatic/contrastive "I (too) / I myself" placed before گفت in poetic word order, and the quoted speech follows: میخواره = "wine‑drinker, drunkard" (self‑designation), کبروی = a proper/name form (here the name or epithet), نام = "name" (i.e. 'called ...'). Grammatically, the sequence uses an older/simplified reporting construction (منم گفت + quoted words) rather than the modern «گفتم: ...»
segments.s605-g21: به روی شهنشاه جام نبید is a nominal clause with a locative fronting: به روی شهنشاه = "in the presence of / before the emperor" (به + روی used idiomatically), جام نبید = "a cup of wine" (جام = cup, نبید = literary word for wine). The copula (بود) is omitted (common in poetry), so the phrase reads as 'a cup of wine (was) before the emperor.'
segments.s605-g22: چو من درکشم یار خواهم گزید begins with چو = "when/if" introducing a temporal/conditional clause; من = subject "I"; درکشم is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning roughly "I draw in / I take (a drink)" (here idiomatically 'when I drink'). یار = "companion/beloved" (object of گزیدن), خواهم + گزید = future construction (خواهم = auxiliary 'I will/want' + گزید used as the lexical verb 'choose') yielding 'I will choose a companion.' Note the classical pairing of خواهم + past stem to express future rather than modern synthetic forms.
segments.s605-g23: به جام اندرون بود می‌پنج من: به جام اندرون بود = "inside the cup there was" (prepositional phrase + locative اندرون + copula بود). می‌پنج is an archaic/poetic compound (here indicating 'five [measures/parts] of wine' or 'five for me') and من is the possessor/emphatic 'for me' — the clause therefore says that inside the cup there were five (portions) belonging to the speaker. The exact lexical makeup of می‌پنج is archaic and idiomatic in classical Persian.
segments.s605-g24: خورم هفت ازین بر سر انجمن: خورم is first‑person present/future of خوردن (here 'I will drink' or 'I drink'), هفت = numeral 'seven', ازین = 'of these' (از = 'from/of', ین = orthographic variant of این), بر سر انجمن = idiom meaning 'in front of / before the assembly' (بر = 'upon/toward', سر = 'head/face' used idiomatically, انجمن = 'assembly'). The clause states the speaker's resolve to drink seven of these in the assembly's presence; note the use of خورم for drinking (خوردن covers both eat and drink) and the poetic prepositional phrase بر سر انجمن.
segments.s605-g25: پس انگه سوی ده روم من به هوش = A sequence of adverbs and direction with a predicative adjective: پس انگه (then/thereupon) sets the temporal sequence; سوی ده روم (toward the Roman village/quadrant) is the directional phrase; من is the nominative 'I' placed before the locative phrase for emphasis; به هوش is a predicative phrase meaning 'in awareness' or 'sober/awake' (here qualifying the subject). Word order is typical epic Persian (temporal adverb → direction → subject → state).
segments.s605-g26: ز من نشنود کس به مستی خروش = A negative statement built around نشنود 'hears/was heard' with the agent/experiencer expressed by ز + من ('from me'). کس is understood in a negative sense ('no one'). به مستی qualifies خروش: 'a cry in drunkenness' or 'a drunken cry'. Note the inverted order (ز من نشنود کس) is poetic: literal 'From me no one heard a drunken cry.'
segments.s605-g27: چنان هفت جام پر از می‌بخورد = 'چنان' (thus/so) modifies the following clause; the object phrase هفت جام پر از می (seven cups full of wine) precedes the verb می‌بخورد. The verb می‌بخورد is an imperfective/form with می- prefix but in epic narrative often reports a past action ('he drank' or 'he would drink'), so render as past 'drank/consumed.'
segments.s605-g28: ازان می‌پرستان برآورد گرد = ازان ('from those/of that') introduces the source (those wine-drinkers). می‌پرستان is the plural noun 'wine-drinkers.' برآورد is a causative/perfective verb 'brought about/caused to rise' and گرد here is a noun 'dust' (a common poetic image for uproar); together they mean 'a dust/commotion arose from those wine-drinkers.'
segments.s605-g29: به دستوری شاه بیرون گذشت = 'به دستوری شاه' is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by the king's order' (دستوری functioning as 'order/command'), followed by بیرون گذشت 'he went out/left' (گذشت here = past 'passed/went'). The construction is straightforward: instrument/agent → verb of motion.
segments.s605-g30: که داند که می‌در تنش چون گذشت = The initial که + داند is a rhetorical 'who knows' (literally 'that he knows' → 'who knows'), introducing a content clause with the second که. می‌در تنش is an older verbless or contracted expression meaning 'the wine/that (mi-) in his body' (می = wine, در + تنش = 'in his body'); چون گذشت 'how it passed' (چون = 'how', گذشت = 'passed/entered'). Together: 'who can say how the wine entered his body.'
segments.s605-g31: وزان جای خرم بیامد به دشت = وزان ('and from that') links back to the preceding episode; جای خرم 'joyful/pleasant place' is the source, بیامد is the past 'he came,' and به دشت is the destination 'into the plain.' The preposition به introduces the place arrived at.
segments.s605-g32: چو در سینه مرد، می‌گرم گشت = چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause; در سینه مرد 'into the man's chest/breast' gives the locus. می‌گرم گشت is a compound where می‌گرم ('becomes warm' / 'grows ardent') with گشت (past 'became') yields 'he became warm/ardent.' The omitted subject is the wine/feeling implied from the previous clause.
segments.s605-g1: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'; بنشست is a simple past (3rd sg.) ‘sat down’. می‌خواست is a past/habitual desiderative ‘he wanted/wished’. از بامداد is an adverbial phrase ('from/at dawn') that belongs with the following action rather than with می‌خواست itself.
segments.s605-g2: بزرگان لشکر is the subject phrase 'the chiefs/nobles of the army'. برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed/went away'. شاد is an adjective used post-verbally to qualify the manner ('joyful' / 'merrily'), a common poetic placement.
segments.s605-g3: بیامد is simple past 'came'. هم‌انگه is an adverb 'at that moment'. یکی is the indefinite 'a/one' (here 'a certain'), preceding the noun as is common in older/poetic Persian. مرد is the noun 'man' and مه here is an epithet or name (literally 'moon' in Persian) attached after the noun.
segments.s605-g4: ورا is the enclitic/old-form object/prepositional pronoun 'to/for him'. میوه آورد is the verb phrase 'brought fruit' (آورد past). چندی ز ده is a source/quantity phrase: چندی 'some/a few' and ز 'from' + ده 'the village', i.e. 'some from the village'.
segments.s605-g5: شتربارها (pl.) 'camel-loads' or caravan cargoes is the subject. The list نار و سیب و بهی enumerates items carried; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. نار here refers to a fruit (classical/poetic usage for pomegranate) rather than its literal 'fire' sense, but that specific fruit sense is not listed in the provided senses, so it is marked absent. سیب 'apple' and بهی 'quince' (بهی as fruit-related) complete the list.
segments.s605-g6: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گل دسته‌ها is 'bunches/tufts of flowers' (گل 'flower' + دسته‌ها 'bundles/bunches'). کرده is a past participle 'made/has made' and شاهنشهی is an adjective/noun 'royal, imperial' placed after the participle in the older/poetic word order — together meaning they had made/arranged them in a royal manner.
segments.s605-g7: جهاندار is the title 'sovereign/king' as subject. چون here introduces a temporal clause ('when' / 'as') and دید is the simple past 'he saw'. بنواختش is a past verb 'he struck/beat/played it' with the enclitic object -ش 'it'; in this context it most naturally means he 'struck/beat (it),' i.e. beat an instrument or struck something to draw attention.
segments.s605-g8: میان یلان is a prepositional phrase 'among the heroes' (میان = among/between, یلان = heroes). پایگه 'station/position' is the predicate noun and ساختش is 'he made/established it' (کرد/ساخت + enclitic -ش 'it'), so the clause says he set it up as a station/stand among the warriors. Word order follows older/poetic S (prepositional) + predicate + verb pattern.

sentences.s605-s17: He roused the steed from the middle of the company and, from the plain, kept galloping up to the foot of the mountain.
sentences.s605-s18: He dismounted, hid the baggage in some spot, loosened (the reins) and slept in the mountain's shade.
sentences.s605-s19: A black crow came down from the mountain, and it plucked out his two eyes from that sleeping‑place.
sentences.s605-s20: They rode on after the company, and there they saw the corpse before the mountain.
sentences.s605-s25: The sovereign of the world’s face grew pale; because of his anxious care for Kubrūy he became full of pain.
sentences.s605-s26: At that moment a cry rose from the court: 'O famed ones, endowed with royal glory and wisdom!'
sentences.s605-s27: It is a disgrace for a scoundrel to hold sway throughout the world, even if he is your famed subordinate.
sentences.s605-s21: The black crow tore his two eyes from his head; his horse stood before him on the road.
sentences.s605-s22: They cried "Go!"; his inferiors burst into an uproar, and from that the assembly and the cups began to boil.
sentences.s605-s23: When Bahram rose from his sleeping quarters, a certain well-wisher came up to him.
sentences.s605-s24: who said that a black crow, in drunkenness, had plucked the bright eye of Kabruy in front of Ragh.
sentences.s605-s5: This very moon — who was accompanied by fruit and fragrance — had beside her a great name.
sentences.s605-s6: He set a cup of wine before the sovereign, and he swallowed two mans at a single draught.
sentences.s605-s7: When the man became joyful at the sight of the king because of those renowned men and that place of celebration.
sentences.s605-s8: Another cup, full and crystal-bright, fell into his heart; from that cup came passion.
sentences.s605-s9: They stretched out their hands before the elders; he rushed toward that cup and sprang to his feet.
sentences.s605-s10: Remembering the king, he seized the cup; I said, 'I am a wine‑drinker called Kabruyi.'
sentences.s605-s11: Before the emperor stood a cup of wine; when I drink, I will choose a companion.
sentences.s605-s12: Within the cup there were five for me; I will drink seven of these before the assembly.
sentences.s605-s13: Then, being awake, I went toward the Roman quarter; from me no one heard a drunken cry.
sentences.s605-s14: Thus he drank seven cups full of wine; from those wine-drinkers a dust (i.e., a tumult) rose.
sentences.s605-s15: At the king's command he went out; who can say how the wine passed into his body?
sentences.s605-s16: And from that joyful place he came into the plain; when it entered the man's breast, he grew hot (became ardent).
sentences.s605-s1: When he sat down, he wanted the army's chiefs to depart at dawn, joyful.
sentences.s605-s2: At that moment a certain man named Mah came and brought him some fruit from the village.
sentences.s605-s3: The camel-loads bore pomegranates, apples and quinces; from bunches of flowers they had fashioned them in royal style.
sentences.s605-s4: When the sovereign saw (this), he struck it and made it a station among the heroes.
